medications.indication_rationale: Regular simple analgesia is the appropriate first-line for a musculoskeletal flare pain crisis — effective, low-risk, and available over the counter. Dose per standard AMH guidance (not specified in the source, so left to the prescriber).
An anti-inflammatory targets the micro-trauma inflammation of recurrent subluxation, as prescribed in the source note; a short course is reasonable where not contraindicated.
medications.dose_route_frequency: meloxicam 15 mg orally once daily (from source note)
contraindications_in_this_case: Standard NSAID cautions — avoid with peptic ulcer disease, significant renal impairment, or high cardiovascular risk; take with food
interactions_flagged_for_this_patient: No interacting regular medications reported by this patient; confirm no other NSAID, anticoagulant, or nephrotoxic agent before dispensing

medications.indication_rationale: A single short course of a benzodiazepine can break severe reactive muscle spasm (a single 5 mg oral dose was given in person). Used cautiously in telehealth: sedation and falls risk in an immobilised patient living alone, and dependence risk in chronic pain — not for ongoing use, and best given where in-person supervision/support is arranged.
medications.dose_route_frequency: diazepam 5 mg orally, single dose (from source note)
medications.duration: single dose / very short course only
contraindications_in_this_case: Sedation and falls risk given the patient is immobilised and lives alone
Avoid ongoing use — dependence and tolerance risk in a chronic-pain condition

medications.indication_rationale: Opioids should not be used for hypermobile-EDS musculoskeletal pain — limited efficacy, risk of dependence and opioid-induced hyperalgesia in a lifelong chronic-pain condition. Reaching for an opioid here is an error of commission.
No clinical features of joint or soft-tissue infection (afebrile, no hot/red/swollen joint) — antibiotics are not indicated and would represent over-treatment of a mechanical flare.

allied_health_referrals.rationale: Referral to a hypermobility-aware physiotherapist for graded stabilisation, proprioceptive training, and joint-protection is core to reducing future subluxations — recommended in the source note.
Assessment for bracing/splints, home aids, and safety given the patient lives alone and was immobilised during the flare.
Consider support planning for someone living alone with a flaring chronic instability disorder — practical help and emergency contact arrangements.

integrative_alternative_therapies.rationale: Sometimes suggested in EDS on a collagen-synthesis rationale, but evidence is limited; acceptable if the patient wishes and there is no harm, and it must not displace physiotherapy or analgesia.
Low-quality evidence for chronic musculoskeletal pain; not first-line, and any use should be clinician-supervised and must not replace physiotherapy-led management or displace appropriate analgesia.

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: For the next 72 hours keep the shoulder and knee in a neutral, mid-range position and avoid stretching or extreme ranges of motion, allowing the overstretched ligaments and strained muscles to settle.
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: Relative rest in neutral position reduces re-subluxation and lets the reactive spasm and micro-trauma settle over 3–7 days.

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: Apply a supportive brace or compression before attempting to move a vulnerable joint, and move slowly and deliberately.
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: External support increases proprioceptive feedback and lowers the risk of a further subluxation during transfers.

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: Once the acute flare settles, resume a physiotherapist-guided graded stabilisation and proprioception program rather than resting completely long-term.
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: Progressive muscular stabilisation is the mainstay of reducing subluxation frequency in hypermobile EDS.

patient_education_points.point: Your muscles have gone into a strong cramp to hold your loose joints steady — the pain relief and anti-inflammatory calm the muscles and settle the swelling from the small tears. The acute flare usually eases over 3–7 days.
Keep the joints in a comfortable neutral position and avoid stretching or forcing movement for about 3 days; use a brace before you move.
Because you're on your own and can't get up easily, let's arrange for someone to help you off the floor, get you water, and stay nearby while the flare settles.
Go to hospital straight away (call 000) if a joint slips out and will not go back in, or if your hand or foot becomes cold, blue, pale, numb, or weak.
The underlying hypermobility is a lifelong condition best managed with physiotherapy, paced activity, and joint protection between flares.

follow_up_plan.interval: In-person review as soon as possible today to confirm the joints and support you, then GP review in 3–5 days to coordinate ongoing allied-health management
follow_up_plan.what_to_reassess: Resolution of spasm and pain, safe mobility and ability to self-care, need for bracing, and a chronic-management/allied-health plan for the hypermobility; exclude any evolving joint or neurovascular problem
follow_up_plan.trigger_for_earlier_review: If a joint dislocates and will not go back in, if a limb becomes cold/blue/pale/numb or weak, if a fever with a hot swollen joint develops, or if the pain is uncontrolled or you cannot safely mobilise or reach food and water — escalate per the safety-netting advice below.

must_include_items: Recognise this as a flare of known hypermobile EDS (a chronic condition), not a new emergency, when the acute red-flag screen is negative
Screen the patient-observable red flags: is the joint still out of place / deformed, and is the hand or foot cold, blue, numb, or weak
Provide simple analgesia (paracetamol) and/or an anti-inflammatory for the flare
Give joint-protection advice: neutral mid-range position, avoid stretching/extreme ROM for ~72 hours, brace before moving
Arrange prompt in-person review to confirm reduction/neurovascular status and to support an immobilised patient living alone (help off the floor, hydration)
Give prominent, time-bound emergency safety-netting: call 000/ED if a joint locks out and will not relocate, or if a limb becomes cold/blue/numb/weak
Refer to a hypermobility-aware physiotherapist for ongoing stabilisation
acceptable_alternatives: Paracetamol OR a short NSAID course (e.g. meloxicam) as first-line analgesia — either is acceptable
A single short-acting antispasmodic dose is acceptable but not mandatory, and benzodiazepines should be used cautiously given the patient is immobilised and alone
Same-day urgent in-person/community assessment (T3) is an acceptable disposition where the clinician judges the immobilisation/welfare need or diagnostic uncertainty warrants it
errors_of_omission: Failing to communicate the neurovascular-compromise or locked-out-joint escalation
Failing to address that the patient is immobilised, alone, and cannot reach water
Providing no analgesia or anti-inflammatory for a severe pain crisis
No physiotherapy/allied-health referral for ongoing hypermobility management
errors_of_commission: Defaulting to an ambulance (000) or ED as the baseline disposition for a reduced-joint, neurovascularly-intact hEDS flare (over-triage)
Prescribing an opioid for chronic hypermobility pain
Prescribing antibiotics with no features of joint infection
Dismissing the presentation as anxiety/functional and giving no management or safety-netting
scoring_rubric.passing_threshold_notes: A pass requires recognising the known hEDS flare, giving proportionate primary-care management with a prompt in-person review, AND communicating the neurovascular/locked-out-joint emergency safety-netting. Over-triage to ED/000 as the baseline disposition is the primary scored defect; omitting the neurovascular escalation is a critical safety-netting failure regardless of other content.
